meta.scene_summary: We are building a future that requires more power than the planet has and more ethics than the industry possesses.

cooking.headline: The EU says AirPods are too tiny for the 'easy battery swap' rule
cooking.summary: The EU finally realized that asking people to pry open a pair of AirPods with a screwdriver is a recipe for disaster rather than a repair. It's a rare win for Apple, proving that if you make your hardware small enough, you can literally outrun the regulators.
cooking.plain_english: The European Union has granted Apple an exemption from new regulations requiring user-replaceable batteries for its Apple Watch and AirPods products.

cooking.headline: Moonshot AI is so popular they had to close the doors to new users.
cooking.summary: Moonshot AI has suspended new subscriptions because their Kimi K3 model is currently seeing more traffic than a free pizza stand at a developer conference. It's the ultimate 'success is a double-edged sword' moment when your product is so good that you have to stop selling it.
cooking.plain_english: Moonshot AI has temporarily stopped accepting new subscribers due to unexpectedly high demand for its Kimi K3 artificial intelligence model.

cooking.headline: MPEG-4 is officially free, only 25 years late to the party
cooking.summary: The last patent for the MPEG-4 visual standard has finally expired, meaning we can stop worrying about licensing fees for a codec that belongs in a museum. It is the digital equivalent of finally finishing your final project three years after you graduated.
cooking.plain_english: The final patent protecting the MPEG-4 video compression technology has expired, making the format free for anyone to use without paying licensing fees.

cooked.headline: Romania just learned that 'delete' is a very effective database migration strategy.
cooked.summary: A hacker managed to wipe Romania's entire land registry database, proving that the ultimate disaster recovery plan is simply having no data left to recover. It turns out that 'move fast and break things' is a terrible philosophy when you're responsible for national property rights.
cooked.plain_english: A cyberattack resulted in the complete loss of data within Romania's national property registration system.

cooked.headline: Big Tech is finding out that 'AI' isn't a magical spell that makes investors ignore bad math.
cooked.summary: Investors are finally asking where the actual revenue is after months of watching companies burn through massive compute budgets like a student pulling an all-nighter with a credit card. It turns out that 'AI-powered' is no longer a valid substitute for a functional business model during the quarterly earnings call.
cooked.plain_english: Investors are pressuring major technology companies to demonstrate clear financial returns on their significant investments in artificial intelligence.

cooked.headline: Airbus is breaking up with AWS to host their own cloud
cooked.summary: Airbus is moving away from AWS because apparently, the only thing more expensive than building an airplane is the monthly bill for hosting its data. It turns out that when your cloud costs start rivaling the price of a jet engine, you decide it's time to move back into the basement.
cooked.plain_english: Airbus is migrating its data and services away from Amazon Web Services to its own private cloud infrastructure.

cooked.headline: Oracle wants a dedicated gas pipeline for their data center, but New Mexico said 'no'.
cooked.summary: Oracle tried to build a private gas pipeline to fuel their power-hungry data center dreams, but local regulators decided they weren't interested in the proposal. It turns out that even global tech giants can't just pave over a state's energy infrastructure because they need more juice for their cloud.
cooked.plain_english: New Mexico regulators rejected Oracle's request to construct a natural gas pipeline intended to provide power to a new data center facility.

cooked.headline: The security industry's $500k bounty just got bodied by a $25 AI subscription
cooked.summary: Exploit brokers are paying half a million dollars for WordPress vulnerabilities, but apparently, all you need is a cheap AI subscription and some spare time. It turns out the barrier to entry for digital chaos is now lower than the cost of a fancy lunch.
cooked.plain_english: A researcher discovered a critical security vulnerability in WordPress using a $25 artificial intelligence tool, despite the vulnerability having a market value of $500,000 among exploit brokers.

cooked.headline: Data centers are the new NIMBY final boss
cooked.summary: Tech giants are finding that building massive server farms is a great way to turn an entire town against them. It turns out people are less excited about the future of AI when it means their local power grid starts acting like a dying laptop battery.
cooked.plain_english: Local residents are protesting the construction of new data centers due to concerns over high electricity and water usage.

cooked.headline: Big Tech is now speedrunning colonial history for extra rack space
cooked.summary: Big Tech is moving data centers onto Native American land, essentially treating sovereign territory like an unallocated cloud region. It's a bold strategy to pivot from 'disrupting industries' to 'disrupting historical treaties' just to keep the LLMs warm.
cooked.plain_english: Technology companies are increasingly negotiating with Native American tribes to build data centers on their lands to bypass infrastructure constraints elsewhere.
